name: Forgery (Documents)
formula: False Document (Fabrication/Alteration/Procured Execution) → Harmful Intent → Document-Type-Calibrated Severity

els.name: Forgery — The Foundational Definition
els.sub: S.463 PPC — making a false document with one of several specified harmful intents
els.desc: Whoever makes any false document or part of a document, with intent to cause damage or injury to the public or any person, or to support any claim or title, or to cause a person to part with property, or to enter into any express or implied contract, or with intent to commit fraud or that fraud may be committed, commits forgery.
els.legal: S.463 itself does not define what makes a document 'false' — that critical definitional work is performed entirely by S.464 (the next element). S.463's own function is to supply the REQUIRED INTENT that transforms the act of making a false document (however defined) into the offence of forgery: one of five disjunctive intent categories — causing damage/injury, supporting a false claim/title, inducing parting with property, inducing entry into a contract, or committing/facilitating fraud generally. Any ONE of these five suffices; they are not cumulative requirements.
els.cite: S.463 PPC — official text. Cross-reference S.464 PPC (the next element) — S.463 supplies the required intent, S.464 supplies the definition of what conduct counts as 'making a false document' in the first place.

landmark.holding: S.477A is confirmed as the FOURTH instance of the clerk/servant employment-position aggravation pattern already traced through S.381 (Theft), S.404 (Misappropriation), and S.408 (Criminal Breach of Trust) — a consistently-applied legislative pattern across four distinct chapters. No verified Pakistani landmark case is logged against S.463–477A specifically as of this build; flagged honestly rather than inventing a citation.

sq.q: A makes a false document, but A's only purpose is private amusement — A has no intent to cause damage, support a claim, induce a property transfer or contract, or commit any fraud whatsoever, and genuinely never intends the document to be shown to or relied upon by anyone. Does A's conduct satisfy S.463?
sq.reveal: No — S.463 requires that the false-document-making be accompanied by at least one of its five specified intent categories. Without any intent to cause damage/injury, support a claim, induce a property transfer or contract, or commit/facilitate fraud, the bare act of making a false document for purely private, non-harmful purposes does not satisfy S.463's intent requirement, regardless of how the document itself might independently satisfy S.464's definitional test for falsity.
sq.cite: S.463 PPC — requiring at least one of five specified harmful intents; the bare act of making a false document without any such intent does not complete the offence.

sq.q: Does S.463 require that the harmful intent actually be realized — that damage actually occur, that the claim actually succeed, or that property actually be parted with — or is the intent itself, at the time of making the false document, sufficient?
sq.reveal: The intent itself, at the time of making the document, is what S.463 requires — the section's text is framed entirely in terms of intent ('with intent to cause damage,' 'with intent to commit fraud or that fraud may be committed'), not in terms of a realized outcome. This is consistent with the general pattern already seen throughout this Code's dishonesty-based offences (Cheating's S.415, for example) where the offender's mental state at the time of the relevant act, not a subsequently realized harmful result, is what completes the offence.
sq.cite: S.463 PPC — intent at the time of making the document as the operative element, not requiring a subsequently realized harmful outcome, consistent with the pattern in S.415 PPC Cheating (already built).

sq.q: How does S.463's 'intent to commit fraud or that fraud may be committed' final limb relate to the other four, more specific intent categories listed earlier in the same section?
sq.reveal: The final limb functions as a broad, catch-all category alongside the four more specific ones — damage/injury, supporting a claim/title, inducing property transfer, and inducing contract entry are all, in a sense, particular species of fraud-adjacent harm, but the final limb's broader 'fraud' language ensures the section captures forgery undertaken for fraudulent purposes that might not neatly fit within one of the four more specifically described categories. This drafting technique (several specific examples followed by a broader catch-all) recurs throughout this Code wherever the legislature wants to ensure comprehensive coverage without having to enumerate every conceivable variant of the underlying wrong.
sq.cite: S.463 PPC's final 'fraud' limb as a broad catch-all alongside four more specific intent categories, ensuring comprehensive coverage of forgery's harmful purposes.

els.dike:
Makes a false document/part of one
∧ intent = (damage/injury to the public/any person ∨ support a claim or title ∨ cause a person to part with property ∨ enter a contract)
→ Forgery

els.name: Making a False Document — The Three Limbs
els.sub: S.464 PPC — fabrication, unauthorized alteration, and procuring execution by deception/incapacity
els.desc: A person makes a false document through any of three limbs: FIRSTLY, dishonestly/fraudulently making, signing, sealing, or executing a document (or making a mark denoting execution) intending it to be believed it was made by someone, or at a time, other than the truth; SECONDLY, without lawful authority, dishonestly/fraudulently altering a document in a material part after its execution; THIRDLY, dishonestly/fraudulently causing someone to sign, seal, execute, or alter a document while knowing that person cannot understand its contents due to unsoundness of mind, intoxication, or deception practised on them. Explanation 1 confirms even signing one's OWN name can be forgery. Explanation 2 confirms forgery can be committed in the name of a fictitious or deceased person.
els.legal: S.464's three limbs map onto three structurally distinct forms of document falsification: FABRICATION (Firstly — creating a document falsely attributed to someone, or falsely dated), UNAUTHORIZED ALTERATION (Secondly — changing an already-genuine document's material content without authority), and PROCURED EXECUTION THROUGH INCAPACITY/DECEPTION (Thirdly — exploiting someone's inability to understand what they're signing). The Code's seventeen Illustrations across the main definition and both Explanations are unusually rich, covering altered letters of credit, unauthorized seals, fraudulently completed blank cheques (both by a stranger and, distinctly, by an authorized agent who exceeds their authorized limit), antedated documents, scratched-out will provisions, converted endorsements, and the genuinely counterintuitive confirmation that signing one's OWN, genuine name can still be forgery if done to create a false impression about authorship.
els.cite: S.464 PPC, both Explanations, and all seventeen Illustrations (the main definition's eleven, Explanation 1's five, and Explanation 2's one) — official text.
els.ll: S.464 PPC and Illustrations — official text

sq.q: A leaves a cheque with B, A's own agent, signed by A but with no sum inserted, specifically authorizing B to fill in any sum up to 10,000 rupees for certain payments. B, exceeding this authority, fraudulently fills in 20,000 rupees instead. Apply S.464's own Illustration (d). Has B committed forgery, despite having been given genuine, real authority to fill in some amount?
sq.reveal: Yes — this is the Code's own Illustration (d) directly: 'B fraudulently fills up the cheque by inserting the sum of twenty thousand rupees. B commits forgery.' B's authority was specifically limited to 10,000 rupees; by fraudulently inserting double that amount, B acted entirely outside the scope of the authority actually granted, satisfying S.464's first limb (creating a document intended to be believed as authorized when B knew it was not, in this respect, actually so authorized). This is a useful contrast to Illustration (c), where a complete stranger with NO authority at all fraudulently fills in a found blank cheque — both constitute forgery, but Illustration (d) confirms even a genuine agent exceeding their actual granted authority commits forgery for the excess.
sq.cite: S.464 PPC Illustration (d) — an authorized agent exceeding the specific scope of granted authority commits forgery, distinguished from but parallel to Illustration (c)'s stranger-with-no-authority scenario.

sq.q: A signs his own genuine name to a bill of exchange, intending that it be believed the bill was drawn by a DIFFERENT person who happens to share the same name as A. Apply S.464's Explanation 1 and its own Illustration. Does A's use of his own real, genuine signature provide any defence against a forgery charge?
sq.reveal: No — this is the Code's own Illustration to Explanation 1 directly: 'A signs his own name to a bill of exchange, intending that it may be believed that the bill was drawn by another person of the same name. A has committed forgery.' Explanation 1 confirms explicitly that 'a man's signature of his own name may amount to forgery' — the wrong lies not in whether the signature is genuinely A's own, but in A's intent to deceive observers into believing the document originates from a different person, even one who happens to share A's name.
sq.cite: S.464 PPC Explanation 1 and its Illustration — using one's own genuine signature can still constitute forgery where the intent is to create a false belief about which specific person of that name authored the document.

sq.q: A draws a bill of exchange upon an entirely fictitious person who does not exist at all, and fraudulently accepts the bill in that fictitious person's name, intending to negotiate it as genuine. Apply S.464's Explanation 2 and its own Illustration. Does the fact that no real person was actually impersonated prevent this from being forgery?
sq.reveal: No — this is the Code's own Illustration to Explanation 2 directly: 'A draws a bill of exchange upon a fictitious person, and fraudulently accepts the bill in the name of such fictitious person with intent to negotiate it. A commits forgery.' Explanation 2 expressly confirms that making a false document in the name of an entirely fictitious person, intending it to be believed the document was made by a real person, independently satisfies forgery — mirroring the same real-or-imaginary-person principle already encountered in S.416's Cheating-by-Personation provision (Cheating module), now confirmed to apply equally to forgery.
sq.cite: S.464 PPC Explanation 2 and its Illustration — forgery in the name of an entirely fictitious person, paralleling the same real-or-imaginary principle already confirmed for S.416 PPC Cheating by Personation (Cheating module).

els.dike:
'Makes a false document' ≡ dishonestly/fraudulently (making ∨ signing ∨ sealing ∨ executing a document, or a mark denoting execution) intending it be believed made by someone/at some time it wasn't
∨ [second limb] ∨ [third limb]
Three alternative routes, any ONE satisfies the making element

els.name: Punishment for Forgery
els.desc: Whoever commits forgery is punished with imprisonment of either description up to 2 years, fine, or both.
els.legal: S.465 is the base, undifferentiated forgery punishment — applicable wherever S.463/464's definitional and intent requirements are satisfied, but none of the further document-type-specific aggravations addressed in S.466 onward apply. This 2-year ceiling sits in a comparable range to several other base property-and-document offences already built in this app, before the chapter's escalating, document-type-specific tiers (reaching as high as life imprisonment for the most significant document categories) take over.

sq.q: A forges a personal letter, falsely attributing authorship to B, purely to embarrass B socially — satisfying S.463's 'damage or injury' intent limb, but the forged document does not fall into any of the specific elevated categories (court records, valuable securities, wills, etc.) addressed by S.466 onward. Apply S.465. What is A's punishment exposure?
sq.reveal: Up to 2 years' imprisonment, fine, or both, under S.465's base tier — since the forged document here is an ordinary personal letter, not one of the specifically elevated document categories (public records, valuable securities, wills) that trigger the higher ceilings in S.466/467 onward. S.465 functions as the residual provision for forgery generally, applicable whenever the more specific, more severely punished document-type categories are not engaged.
sq.cite: S.465 PPC as the residual base provision for forgery generally, applicable where none of S.466 onward's specific document-type aggravations are engaged.

sq.q: Does S.465 itself specify which kinds of documents fall within its scope, or does it apply to forgery of any document not otherwise specifically addressed by a more particular provision later in this chapter?
sq.reveal: S.465's text simply punishes 'whoever commits forgery' without itself enumerating any specific document categories — it functions as the general, catch-all punishment provision for forgery as defined in S.463/464, with the more specific document-type categories (court/public records in S.466, valuable securities/wills in S.467, and the further specific-intent provisions in S.468/469) layered on top as more severely punished special cases. Where a forged document does not fall within any of those more specific categories, S.465's general provision remains the operative one.
sq.cite: S.465 PPC — a general, document-type-unspecified provision, with S.466 onward supplying more specific, more severely punished categories layered on top.

sq.q: Compare S.465's 2-year ceiling to S.466's 7-year ceiling for forgery of court records or public registers. What does this differential suggest about how the legislature weighs the significance of the document type forged, independent of the forger's specific underlying purpose?
sq.reveal: The substantial increase from S.465 to S.466 reflects that the LEGISLATURE has identified certain document categories — official court records, public registers, public-servant certificates — as warranting elevated protection specifically because of their institutional and evidentiary significance to the broader legal and administrative system, independent of whatever specific further purpose (damage, fraud, property-transfer inducement) the forger may have had under S.463. This confirms the chapter's structure layers document-type significance as an independent escalating factor on top of the underlying forgery offence, similar to how other chapters in this Code layer aggravating factors like value thresholds or named protected categories on top of their own base offences.
sq.cite: S.465 PPC vs S.466 PPC — document-type significance as an independent escalating factor, reflecting the institutional/evidentiary importance of certain document categories regardless of the forger's specific underlying purpose.

els.name: Forgery of Court Records/Public Registers; Forgery of Valuable Securities, Wills, and Related Instruments
els.sub: S.466/467 PPC — the two most severely punished document categories
els.desc: S.466 punishes forging a document purporting to be a Court of Justice record/proceeding, a birth/baptism/marriage/burial register, a public servant's official register or certificate, an authority to institute/defend a suit or confess judgment, or a power of attorney — with up to 7 years plus fine. S.467 punishes forging a document purporting to be a valuable security, a will, an authority to adopt a son, or an authority to make/transfer a valuable security or receive money/property/securities under one — with imprisonment for life, or up to 10 years, plus fine, the highest ceiling in this entire sub-cluster (with a special Schedule II sub-note: where the forged valuable security is specifically a Central Government promissory note, cognizability is elevated for that particular document type).
els.legal: S.466 protects the institutional integrity of court records, vital-event registers, and official public-servant certifications/authorities — documents whose falsification corrupts the legal and administrative system's own evidentiary foundations. S.467 protects an even more severely punished category: valuable securities, wills, and adoption authorities — documents that directly govern the transfer or disposition of significant property or family-status rights. S.467's life-imprisonment ceiling, the highest in this sub-cluster, reflects the potentially enormous and often irreversible financial or familial harm that forging a will, adoption authority, or valuable security can cause, compared to the more institutional/procedural harm S.466 addresses.
els.cite: S.466 and S.467 PPC — official text.

sq.q: A forges a document purporting to be an official birth register entry, intending to falsely establish someone's claimed age for an unrelated legal purpose. Separately, B forges a document purporting to be a will, falsely altering the distribution of a deceased person's estate. Apply S.466 and S.467 respectively. Compare A and B's punishment exposure.
sq.reveal: A faces up to 7 years under S.466, since a birth register is specifically named among that section's protected document categories. B faces a substantially higher exposure — life imprisonment, or up to 10 years — under S.467, since a will is specifically named among ITS protected categories, reflecting the legislature's judgment that forged wills (directly determining the disposition of a deceased person's entire estate) warrant the most severe punishment tier in this sub-cluster, exceeding even the institutional harm of a falsified public register.
sq.cite: S.466 PPC (7 years, public records/registers) vs S.467 PPC (life/10 years, wills/valuable securities/adoption authorities) — reflecting the more severe, often irreversible familial/financial harm S.467's categories address.

sq.q: Why might S.467 specifically single out Central Government promissory notes for a cognizability enhancement within Schedule II, when the underlying PPC punishment ceiling for forging any valuable security under S.467 (including such a note) remains the same regardless of the specific type of security forged?
sq.reveal: This reflects a procedural (CrPC Schedule II), not substantive (PPC), distinction — the PPC's own S.467 punishment ceiling applies uniformly to all valuable securities, wills, and adoption authorities it covers, but the CrPC's Schedule II procedural classification can and does draw finer distinctions for specific document sub-types within that same substantive category, presumably reflecting a judgment that forgery of Government-issued financial instruments specifically (as opposed to private valuable securities generally) warrants the procedural advantage of allowing police to arrest without first obtaining a warrant, given the potential systemic financial/sovereign-credibility harm such forgery could cause.
sq.cite: S.467 PPC's uniform substantive punishment vs Schedule II's specific cognizability enhancement for Central Government promissory notes — a procedural distinction layered on top of, not altering, the underlying substantive punishment.

sq.q: Does S.467's protection of 'an authority to adopt a son' suggest this provision was drafted with reference to a specific personal-law or religious adoption framework, and how might this interact with the General Exceptions or other personal-status provisions already encountered elsewhere in this app?
sq.reveal: This is a fair observation worth noting honestly rather than overstating: S.467's specific reference to 'an authority to adopt a son' likely reflects the 1860 drafting context, where formal authorizations to adopt (particularly significant under certain personal-law traditions where adoption carries specific inheritance and family-status consequences) were a recognized category of legally significant document. This app has not independently built out the personal-law adoption framework itself, so the precise modern interaction between this specific S.467 protection and Pakistan's current adoption-law landscape is not something this module can confidently elaborate on without risking unverified claims — flagged honestly as a boundary of this module's current scope rather than an area to speculate about.
sq.cite: S.467 PPC's adoption-authority protection — honestly flagged as reflecting historical personal-law context, with the precise modern interaction left unaddressed rather than speculated upon.

els.dike:
S.466 — forging a (court record ∨ public register ∨ official certificate ∨ authority to sue/confess judgment)
S.467 — forging (valuable securities ∨ wills ∨ related instruments) → higher tier
The TYPE of document forged, not just the act of forging, drives the punishment band

els.name: Forgery for the Purpose of Cheating; Forgery for the Purpose of Harming Reputation
els.sub: S.468/469 PPC — two purpose-specific aggravations layered on the base forgery offence
els.desc: S.468 punishes forgery intending that the forged document be used for cheating, with up to 7 years plus fine. S.469 punishes forgery intending (or knowing it likely) that the forged document will harm someone's reputation, with up to 3 years plus fine.
els.legal: These two sections layer specific FURTHER PURPOSE aggravations onto the base forgery offence, independent of the document-type categories addressed in S.466/467 — a forged document of any type, used or intended specifically to facilitate cheating (already extensively built in the Cheating module), triggers S.468's substantially higher 7-year ceiling regardless of whether the document itself falls into one of S.466/467's elevated categories. S.469's reputation-harm purpose, by contrast, carries a comparatively modest 3-year ceiling — still above S.465's bare 2-year base, but well below S.468's cheating-purpose tier, suggesting the legislature treated facilitating financial/property-based cheating as a more serious aggravating purpose than facilitating reputational harm specifically, even though both represent purpose-based escalations above ordinary forgery.
els.cite: S.468 and S.469 PPC — official text. Cross-reference Cheating module S.415–420 — the underlying cheating offence S.468's forgery-for-cheating provision is specifically calibrated to facilitate.

sq.q: A forges a document specifically intending to use it to cheat Z out of property, following the same cheating mechanics already built in the Cheating module (S.415/420). Apply S.468. Does A face liability under both S.468 (forgery for cheating) and the underlying Cheating provisions themselves, or does one displace the other?
sq.reveal: In principle, A's conduct could independently satisfy both S.468 (forgery specifically intending the document's use for cheating) and the underlying cheating offence itself (S.420, if the cheating scheme actually proceeds to induce delivery of property) — these address genuinely distinct acts (the forgery itself, and the subsequent cheating using that forged document), and nothing in either section's text suggests one is meant to displace or subsume the other. A could face cumulative liability for both the forgery (S.468) and, if the cheating itself is carried out, the separate cheating offence (S.420, Cheating module), since they punish different stages and aspects of what might be a single overall fraudulent scheme.
sq.cite: S.468 PPC and S.420 PPC (Cheating module) — potentially cumulative liability for the forgery itself and the subsequent cheating using that forged document, addressing distinct stages of an overall scheme.

sq.q: Why might the legislature have set S.468's cheating-purpose ceiling (7 years) more than double S.469's reputation-harm-purpose ceiling (3 years), despite both being purpose-specific aggravations layered on the same base forgery offence?
sq.reveal: This is worth noting as an observed calibration rather than a definitively explained one: cheating-purpose forgery typically aims at extracting property or financial value through the forged document (mirroring the property-focused harm already extensively addressed throughout this Code's property-offence chapters), while reputation-harm-purpose forgery aims at a different, non-property kind of harm. The legislature's significantly higher ceiling for the cheating-purpose variant may reflect a judgment that property/financial harm facilitated by forgery warrants more severe treatment than reputational harm facilitated by forgery, though the text itself does not state this rationale explicitly.
sq.cite: S.468 PPC's more-than-double ceiling over S.469 PPC — an observed calibration potentially reflecting greater concern with property/financial harm over reputational harm, though not explicitly stated in either section's text.

sq.q: Does S.469 require that the reputation harm actually occur, or that the offender specifically and personally intend that harm, or is knowledge of mere likelihood sufficient on its own?
sq.reveal: S.469's text covers forgery undertaken either 'intending that the document forged shall harm the reputation of any party, OR knowing that it is likely to be used for that purpose' — this disjunctive structure means knowledge of likely reputation-harming use, even without specific personal intent to cause that harm, is independently sufficient. This mirrors the same intent-or-knowledge disjunctive structure recurring throughout this Code's mental-state requirements (S.415 Cheating, S.425 Mischief, and numerous others already built), confirming a consistently applied drafting convention rather than an isolated feature unique to S.469.
sq.cite: S.469 PPC's disjunctive 'intending... or knowing... likely' structure — knowledge of likely reputation-harming use independently sufficient, consistent with the recurring intent-or-knowledge pattern throughout this Code.

els.dike:
S.468 — forgery ∧ intending the document be used for cheating → ≤7 years ∧ fine
S.469 — forgery ∧ (intending ∨ knowing likely) to harm reputation → ≤3 years
Same act, different downstream purpose, very different ceilings

els.name: Forged Document — Definition; Using a Forged Document as Genuine
els.sub: S.470/471 PPC — the resulting-document definition and the downstream-use offence
els.desc: S.470 simply designates any false document made wholly or partly by forgery as 'a forged document.' S.471 punishes fraudulently or dishonestly using as genuine any document known or reasonably believed to be a forged document — with the SAME punishment as would apply to the original forgery of that specific document (a special Schedule II sub-note again applies where the forged document is specifically a Central Government promissory note, again elevating cognizability for that document type).
els.legal: S.471 is a deliberately derivative provision: rather than fixing its own independent punishment ceiling, it directs that the user of a forged document faces 'the same manner as if he had forged such document' — meaning the actual punishment depends entirely on which underlying forgery provision (S.465's base tier, S.466's public-record tier, or S.467's valuable-security/will tier) the specific forged document would have triggered had the user been the original forger. This structure ensures a person who knowingly uses a forged will, for example, faces the same severe S.467 exposure as the original forger, rather than some separately-fixed, potentially lesser punishment — closing a potential gap where downstream users of forged documents might otherwise escape with lighter punishment than the original forger despite causing comparable harm through the document's actual use.
els.cite: S.470 and S.471 PPC — official text. Cross-reference S.465/466/467 PPC (this same module) — S.471's punishment is entirely derivative of whichever of these provisions the specific forged document would trigger.

sq.q: A knowingly and fraudulently uses, as genuine, a document A knows to be a forged will (the kind of document covered by S.467's life-imprisonment/10-year tier) — but A was not the person who originally forged it; A merely received it from someone else and chose to use it. Apply S.471. What is A's punishment exposure?
sq.reveal: A faces the identical punishment exposure as the original forger would have faced under S.467 specifically — life imprisonment, or up to 10 years, plus fine — since S.471 directs that using a forged document as genuine is punished 'in the same manner as if he had forged such document.' A's own non-involvement in the original forgery act itself does not reduce A's exposure; the severity is determined entirely by which underlying forgery category (here, S.467's will/valuable-security tier) the specific document would have triggered.
sq.cite: S.471 PPC — punishment entirely derivative of the underlying document's forgery category (here S.467's severe tier), regardless of the user's non-involvement in the original forgery act.

sq.q: Why might the legislature have structured S.471 as a derivative provision (borrowing the underlying forgery section's punishment) rather than simply fixing a single, independent punishment ceiling for using any forged document as genuine, regardless of document type?
sq.reveal: A single fixed ceiling would either substantially under-punish users of the most severe forged-document categories (wills, valuable securities under S.467) relative to the original forgers, or substantially over-punish users of the least severe categories (ordinary forged documents under S.465) relative to the harm actually involved. The derivative structure ensures the punishment for KNOWING USE remains proportional to the same document-type-based severity calibration already built into S.465–467, rather than collapsing that careful gradation into a single undifferentiated figure for the downstream-use offence.
sq.cite: S.471 PPC's derivative structure — preserving the same document-type-based severity gradation already established in S.465–467, rather than collapsing it into a single undifferentiated punishment figure.

sq.q: Does S.471 require that the user know with certainty that the document is forged, or is the same 'reason to believe' standard already encountered elsewhere in this Code (such as S.411 Receiving Stolen Property) sufficient here too?
sq.reveal: S.471's text covers using a document 'which he knows OR HAS REASON TO BELIEVE to be a forged document' — the same objective 'reason to believe' standard already built into General Explanations S.26 and applied in S.411 Receiving Stolen Property (Receiving module) applies here as well. A user who lacks actual certainty but has objectively sufficient cause to believe the document is forged still satisfies S.471's mental-state requirement, mirroring the same pattern of consistent mental-state drafting recurring throughout this Code's dishonesty-based offences.
sq.cite: S.471 PPC's 'knows or has reason to believe' standard — the same objective standard already built into General Explanations S.26 and applied in S.411 PPC Receiving Stolen Property (Receiving module).

els.dike:
S.470 — false document made wholly/partly by forgery ≡ 'a forged document' (pure definition)
S.471 — (fraudulently ∨ dishonestly) uses as genuine a document (known ∨ reasonably believed) forged
→ SAME punishment as if the user had forged it themselves — using is treated as equivalent to making

els.name: Counterfeiting Seals, Plates, or Authenticating Devices; Possessing Forged Documents with Intent to Use
els.desc: S.472 punishes making/counterfeiting (or possessing with knowledge) a seal/plate/instrument intended for committing S.467-punishable forgery — life imprisonment or up to 7 years, plus fine. S.473 punishes the identical conduct intended for any OTHER forgery in this chapter (not S.467-punishable) — up to 7 years, plus fine. S.474 punishes possessing a document known to be forged, intending its use as genuine, where the document falls within S.466's or S.467's categories — up to 7 years (S.466-type) or life/up to 7 years (S.467-type), plus fine. S.475/476 punish counterfeiting an authenticating device/mark, or possessing material bearing such a counterfeited device, for S.467-type documents (S.475, same severe tier) or other documents (S.476, up to 7 years, explicitly non-bailable per Schedule II).
els.legal: These five sections together form a comprehensive PREPARATORY-INSTRUMENT cluster, criminalising the tools and materials of forgery (counterfeit seals, plates, authenticating devices, and forged documents held in preparation for use) independently of any completed act of forgery or downstream use — mirroring the same inchoate-liability logic already seen in S.398 (possessing a deadly weapon during an attempted robbery, Robbery & Dacoity module) and the Abetment chapter's concealment provisions. The consistent S.467-vs-other-forgery bifurcation running through S.472/473 and S.475/476 confirms the legislature carried its document-type severity calibration (the most protected category being valuable securities/wills/adoption authorities) through even to the preparatory-instrument stage, not merely to the completed forgery or its downstream use.
els.cite: S.472, S.473, S.474, S.475, and S.476 PPC — official text. Cross-reference S.398 PPC (Robbery & Dacoity module) — the same preparatory-instrument/possession-based inchoate liability logic recurring here.

sq.q: A makes a counterfeit seal specifically intending it to be used for forging documents that would fall under S.467's severe tier (valuable securities, wills). B makes an identical-looking counterfeit seal, but specifically intending it for forging an ordinary personal document that would only trigger S.465's base tier. Apply S.472 and S.473 respectively. Compare A and B's exposure.
sq.reveal: A faces life imprisonment, or up to 7 years, under S.472, since A's intended use specifically targets S.467-punishable forgery (the most severe document category). B faces only up to 7 years under S.473's lower ceiling (notably, without the life-imprisonment option), since B's intended use targets forgery NOT punishable under S.467 specifically. Despite the seals themselves being functionally identical objects, the legislature calibrates the preparatory-instrument offence's severity entirely by reference to the intended downstream forgery's own severity category — carrying the document-type calibration all the way back to the instrument-preparation stage.
sq.cite: S.472 PPC (S.467-intended use, life/7 years) vs S.473 PPC (other forgery intended, 7 years only, no life-imprisonment option) — the document-type severity calibration carried through to the preparatory-instrument stage.

sq.q: A is found in possession of a document A knows to be forged — specifically a forged power of attorney (one of S.466's named categories) — with clear intent to use it as genuine. Apply S.474. Which specific tier within S.474 applies, and what is A's exposure?
sq.reveal: Since a power of attorney is specifically named among S.466's protected categories, A falls under S.474's S.466-linked tier: up to 7 years, plus fine — rather than S.474's other tier (linked to S.467's categories), which would carry the more severe life-imprisonment/10-year exposure applicable specifically to forged valuable securities, wills, or adoption authorities. S.474 thus mirrors the same two-tier document-type structure already established by S.466/467 themselves, simply applied to the possession-with-intent-to-use stage rather than the forgery or downstream-use stages.
sq.cite: S.474 PPC's S.466-linked tier (7 years) applying specifically to a forged power of attorney, mirroring S.466's own document-category structure at the possession stage.

sq.q: Why might the legislature have separately criminalised the counterfeiting of authenticating DEVICES OR MARKS (S.475/476) as distinct from the counterfeiting of SEALS, PLATES, OR INSTRUMENTS (S.472/473), rather than treating both under a single unified provision?
sq.reveal: Seals, plates, and instruments (S.472/473) are typically physical tools used to directly produce or execute a forged document or signature, while authenticating devices or marks (S.475/476) are more specifically about replicating the distinctive markings, watermarks, or other authentication features that give a document or material the APPEARANCE of genuineness once forged — a related but conceptually distinct preparatory act focused on the verification/authentication layer rather than the document-creation act itself. Separating these into distinct provisions allows the legislature to precisely target each distinct stage and method of forgery-preparation, consistent with the granular, methodical structure already seen throughout this entire chapter.
sq.cite: S.472/473 PPC (seals/plates/instruments, document-creation tools) vs S.475/476 PPC (authenticating devices/marks, appearance-of-genuineness tools) — distinct preparatory stages and methods warranting separate, precisely targeted provisions.

els.dike:
S.472 — (makes/counterfeits ∨ possesses with knowledge) a seal/plate/instrument intended for S.467-tier forgery → life ∨ ≤7 years ∧ fine
S.473 — IDENTICAL conduct, intended for a lesser tier of forgery → correspondingly lower punishment
The tool's intended use, not the tool itself, sets the tier

els.name: Fraudulent Cancellation, Destruction, or Defacement of a Will, Adoption Authority, or Valuable Security
els.sub: S.477 PPC — the destructive counterpart to S.467's forgery protection
els.desc: Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly, or with intent to cause damage or injury to the public or any person, cancels, destroys, or defaces (or attempts to, or secretes or attempts to secrete) any document that is or purports to be a will, adoption authority, or valuable security, or commits mischief in respect of such a document, is punished with imprisonment for life, or up to 7 years, plus fine.
els.legal: S.477 is the structural mirror-image of S.467: where S.467 punishes FORGING (creating a false version of) a will, adoption authority, or valuable security, S.477 punishes DESTROYING, CANCELLING, DEFACING, or SECRETING a genuine one of the same document categories — both routes (fabricating a false instance, or destroying/hiding a genuine one) achieve a comparable practical harm to the rightful holder's interests in that document, and the legislature calibrates both to a comparably severe punishment tier (life imprisonment available in both). The explicit 'commits mischief in respect to such document' language directly cross-references the Mischief chapter already built, confirming this section sits at a genuine intersection between the Forgery cluster and the Mischief framework, similar to how S.461/462's receptacle-breaking provisions sat at the intersection of dishonesty-based and destruction-based offence categories.
els.cite: S.477 PPC — official text. Cross-reference S.467 PPC (this same module) — the structural mirror-image relationship between forging and destroying the same protected document categories. Cross-reference Mischief module S.425 — the explicit 'commits mischief' cross-reference.

sq.q: A, who stands to inherit less under a deceased relative's genuine will than under the laws of intestate succession that would apply if no will existed, dishonestly destroys the genuine will specifically to prevent its terms from taking effect. Apply S.477. Compare this conceptually to S.467's forgery offence — is destroying a genuine will treated by the Code as comparably serious to forging a false one?
sq.reveal: Yes — both S.477 (destroying a genuine will) and S.467 (forging a false will) carry the identical severe punishment ceiling: life imprisonment, or imprisonment of either description up to a similar high range. A's destruction of the genuine will to defeat its terms achieves a practically equivalent harm to forging a false will that achieves the same end (defeating the true testamentary intent) — the Code's parallel, comparably severe treatment of both routes confirms the legislature recognised that creating a false instrument and destroying a genuine one are functionally equivalent paths to the same underlying wrong against testamentary and property-disposition integrity.
sq.cite: S.477 PPC vs S.467 PPC — comparably severe punishment for both forging a false will/valuable security and destroying a genuine one, reflecting their functionally equivalent harm to testamentary/property-disposition integrity.

sq.q: A merely attempts to destroy a will, but is interrupted before actually completing the destruction — the will survives intact. Apply S.477's own text. Does the failed attempt still satisfy the section?
sq.reveal: Yes — S.477's text explicitly covers not only actually cancelling, destroying, or defacing the document, but also 'attempts to cancel, destroy or deface' it, an explicit inchoate-liability extension built directly into the section's own definition (rather than requiring resort to the general S.511 attempt framework, Stages of Crime foundation module). A's interrupted attempt, even though the will ultimately survives intact, independently satisfies S.477 through this explicit attempt-coverage.
sq.cite: S.477 PPC's explicit 'attempts to cancel, destroy or deface' language — an inchoate-liability extension built directly into the section's own text, not requiring resort to the general S.511 attempt framework.

sq.q: Why might S.477 specifically include 'secretes or attempts to secrete' the document as an additional, distinct route to liability, alongside cancelling, destroying, or defacing it?
sq.reveal: Secreting (hiding) a will or valuable security achieves a practically similar harm to actually destroying it — if the document cannot be found or produced when needed, its legal effect is frustrated just as completely as if it had been physically destroyed, even though the document itself remains intact somewhere. Including secretion as an independent route ensures the section captures this practically-equivalent-but-physically-different method of defeating the document's effect, rather than allowing a person to escape liability simply by hiding rather than destroying the document, when the practical harm to the rightful parties is functionally identical either way.
sq.cite: S.477 PPC's 'secretes' limb — capturing the practically equivalent harm of hiding a document, since an undiscoverable document is functionally as defeated as a destroyed one.

els.dike:
(Fraudulently ∨ dishonestly ∨ intent to damage/injure) ∧ (cancels ∨ destroys ∨ defaces ∨ attempts to ∨ secretes/attempts to secrete)
∧ document that IS ∨ PURPORTS TO BE (a will ∨ adoption authority ∨ valuable security)
Attempt and secretion are treated on the same footing as the completed act

els.name: Falsification of Accounts
els.sub: S.477A PPC — the chapter-closing, employment-position-specific provision
els.desc: Whoever, being (or employed as) a clerk, officer, or servant, wilfully and with intent to defraud, destroys, alters, mutilates, or falsifies any book/paper/writing/valuable security/account belonging to or possessed by their employer (or received on the employer's behalf), or makes/abets a false entry, or omits/alters/abets the omission of any material particular in such records, is punished with imprisonment up to 7 years, fine, or both. The Explanation confirms a charge under this section need only allege a general intent to defraud, without naming a specific intended victim, a specific sum, or a specific date.
els.legal: S.477A (inserted by the 1895 Criminal Law Amendment Act, distinctly later than the original 1860 chapter) closes the Forgery sub-cluster with an employment-position-specific provision mirroring the same clerk/servant aggravation pattern already seen throughout this app (Theft's S.381, Misappropriation's S.404, Criminal Breach of Trust's S.408) — here applied specifically to falsification of business records and accounts rather than misappropriation of property directly. The Explanation's relaxed pleading standard (no need to specify a particular victim, sum, or date) reflects a practical recognition that systematic account falsification, by its nature, often involves an ongoing pattern of small, hard-to-precisely-pin-down entries rather than a single, easily dated and quantified act — allowing prosecution to proceed on proof of a general fraudulent scheme without requiring the kind of specific particularity that might be available for, and required of, a single discrete forgery act.
els.cite: S.477A PPC and its Explanation, inserted by the Criminal Law Amendment Act 1895 — official text. Cross-reference S.381 PPC (Theft module), S.404 PPC (Misappropriation module), S.408 PPC (Criminal Breach of Trust module) — the same recurring clerk/servant employment-position aggravation pattern, now confirmed a fourth time, applied here to account falsification specifically.
els.ll: S.477A PPC and Explanation — official text

sq.q: A is an accounting clerk who, over an extended period, systematically makes numerous small false entries in their employer's books, wilfully and with intent to defraud, but the prosecution cannot identify one single specific date, one single specific sum, or one single specific intended victim tied to any particular individual falsified entry — only the overall pattern of systematic falsification is clearly established. Apply S.477A's Explanation. Does this evidentiary gap prevent a successful charge?
sq.reveal: No — S.477A's own Explanation directly addresses this exact scenario: 'it shall be sufficient in any charge under this section to allege a general intent to defraud without naming any particular person intended to be defrauded or specifying any particular sum of money intended to be the subject of the fraud, or any particular day on which the offence was committed.' The prosecution can proceed on proof of the general fraudulent scheme and intent, without needing to pin down the kind of granular, individually-specified detail that might otherwise be required, reflecting a practical recognition of how systematic account falsification typically actually occurs and is typically actually detected.
sq.cite: S.477A PPC Explanation — relaxed pleading standard permitting prosecution based on a general fraudulent scheme/intent, without requiring specification of a particular victim, sum, or date.

sq.q: How does S.477A's clerk/servant employment-position requirement compare to the identical structural requirement already built into S.381 (Theft), S.404 (Misappropriation), and S.408 (Criminal Breach of Trust)? Is this now confirmed as a clean, recurring pattern across this app's property-and-document-offence modules?
sq.reveal: Yes — S.477A is now the FOURTH confirmed instance of the identical employment-position aggravation pattern: in each case, the legislature specifically targets the breach of trust inherent in an employment relationship where the employee is given some form of access to or responsibility for the employer's property, accounts, or records, and calibrates an elevated punishment specifically for exploitation of that access. This is a genuinely valuable piece of cross-module synthesis — recognising this as one consistently-applied legislative pattern across four otherwise distinct chapters (Theft, Misappropriation, Criminal Breach of Trust, and now Forgery/Account-Falsification), rather than four coincidentally similar but unrelated provisions, is exactly the kind of structural insight that strengthens an exam answer.
sq.cite: S.477A PPC as the fourth confirmed instance of the clerk/servant employment-position aggravation pattern, alongside S.381 PPC (Theft), S.404 PPC (Misappropriation), and S.408 PPC (Criminal Breach of Trust) — a consistently-applied legislative pattern across four distinct chapters.

sq.q: Does S.477A require that the falsified records actually belong to the employer personally, or could records belonging to a third party, but held by the employer (and falsified by the employee in that context), also satisfy the section?
sq.reveal: S.477A's text covers records 'which belongs to or is in the possession of his employer, or has been received by him for or on behalf of his employer' — this language extends beyond records the employer personally owns, to records merely in the employer's possession or received on the employer's behalf, which would naturally include records belonging to a third party (such as a client) but held or managed by the employer's business. An employee who falsifies such third-party-owned but employer-held records, in the course of their employment, would still satisfy S.477A's scope, since the section's protective concern extends to the full range of records the employer's business handles, not merely those the employer personally and exclusively owns.
sq.cite: S.477A PPC's 'belongs to or is in the possession of... or has been received... on behalf of' language — extending coverage to third-party-owned records held or managed by the employer, not merely employer-owned records.

els.dike:
(Being ∨ employed as) a clerk/officer/servant ∧ wilfully ∧ intent to defraud
∧ (destroys ∨ alters ∨ mutilates ∨ falsifies) a (book ∨ paper ∨ writing ∨ valuable security ∨ account) belonging to/possessed by their employer
Employment relationship is a threshold element — an outsider doing the identical act falls under ordinary Forgery, not this section

landmark: S.477A is confirmed as the FOURTH instance of the clerk/servant employment-position aggravation pattern already traced through S.381 (Theft), S.404 (Misappropriation), and S.408 (Criminal Breach of Trust) — a consistently-applied legislative pattern across four distinct chapters. No verified Pakistani landmark case is logged against S.463–477A specifically as of this build; flagged honestly rather than inventing a citation.
summary: This module covers the Forgery sub-cluster (S.463–477A) of the broader Chapter XVIII ‘Of Offences Relating to Documents and to Trade or Property Marks.’ It builds the foundational two-part definition (S.463’s required intent, S.464’s three-limb falsity definition, all seventeen original Illustrations including the counterintuitive confirmation that signing one’s own genuine name can still be forgery); the base punishment (S.465); the two most severely punished document categories — court/public records (S.466) and valuable securities/wills/adoption authorities (S.467, the highest ceiling in this sub-cluster); two further purpose-specific aggravations for cheating and reputation-harm (S.468/469); the derivative downstream-use offence (S.470/471); the comprehensive preparatory-instrument cluster covering counterfeit seals, plates, and authenticating devices (S.472–476); the destructive mirror-image of S.467’s forgery protection (S.477); and the chapter-closing, employment-position-specific account-falsification provision (S.477A).
